# On Your Best Behavior: How to Nudge Ethically

![](https://www.behavioraleconomics.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/Nudge.png)

*By Alain Samson*

*This article discusses ethical considerations surrounding behavioral nudges by focusing on three core areas: Goals (of nudgers), Autonomy (of the nudged), and Effects (of nudges). The framework prompts practitioners to evaluate the alignment of nudgers’ intentions with the best interests of the nudged, the preservation of individual autonomy, and the evaluation of costs and benefits associated with behavioral interventions. It offers a set of practical questions for professionals seeking to build an ethically sound nudge practice, emphasizing the importance of a nuanced understanding of the backgrounds and needs of nudge targets, tailored interventions, and continuous monitoring.*

### Introduction

If you’ve ever come across online subscription options that highlight the most popular choice or a fruit basket placed next to the cash register in a cafeteria, you’ve encountered what behavioral scientists call a “nudge.”

[Nudging](https://www.nytimes.com/2008/08/24/books/review/Friedman-t.html) is a practice that uses tweaks in the way choices are presented to people to steer behavior, such as changing decision [defaults](https://www.behavioraleconomics.com/resources/mini-encyclopedia-of-be/default-optionsetting/), asking people to [precommit](https://www.behavioraleconomics.com/resources/mini-encyclopedia-of-be/precommitment/) to an action, or making choices more [salient](https://thedecisionlab.com/biases/salience-bias/). The use of nudges has arguably become the most prominent technique in applied behavioral science. Nudge projects have produced many [success stories](https://www.theguardian.com/politics/2018/nov/10/nudge-unit-pushed-way-private-sector-behavioural-insights-team), but the practice has also raised important [concerns](http://dx.doi.org/10.1561/105.00000092) about imposing specific ideas about what’s best for individual decision makers, influencing choices on a non-conscious level, and potentially producing unforeseen and unwanted outcomes.

These [critical debates](https://www.nytimes.com/2013/08/09/opinion/brooks-the-nudge-debate.html), reinforced by a lack of regulation and recent cases of [academic misconduct](https://www.nature.com/articles/d41586-021-02421-w), make it clear that nudge practice is in need of ethical guidelines. This is not just crucial for behavioral scientists. Whether you design a new banking app, implement Covid-19 policy, or sell men’s fashion, your work probably influences choices. Some of this may occur in the traditional way through economic incentives (e.g. providing discounts), information provision (e.g. communicating benefits), or mandates (e.g. prohibiting an action), but it may also involve nudges. This article briefly discusses the three main problem areas associated with nudging—Goals, Autonomy, and Effects—and offers practical questions that can help you build ethical nudge application.

### Goals (of the Nudger)

To build ethical nudge practice we first need understand *who*does the nudging and, most importantly, *why* it occurs—the nudger’s [intentions and goals](https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2013.00972).

Nudgers’ motives can be selfish (nudges serve the interest of the nudger), paternalistic (the nudger acts in the interest of the nudged), or pro-social (individuals are nudged to help others). In addition, nudges can benefit the nudger, the nudged, others (another individual or group of people), or the collective (i.e. society as a whole, including the nudged).

| **Motive** | **Primary Beneficiary** | **Examples** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Selfish | Nudger | Boosting product sales or political power |
| Paternalistic | Nudged | Increasing retirement savings or health |
| Pro-social | Others | Encouraging organ donations or charitable giving |
| Pro-social | Collective | Reducing spread of diseases or CO2 emissions |

How you ethically evaluate these nudges depends on the [perspective](https://www.bbc.co.uk/ethics/introduction/) you adopt. Selfish nudges, for example, may ignore the nudged’s preferences. But does a selfish motive matter if the nudge doesn’t cause harm or is even mutually beneficial? In addition, some public opinion research suggests that nudges classified as benefiting society are significantly [less accepted](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007%2Fs13164-015-0263-2) than those which benefit the nudged as an individual. Does this make pro-social nudges that don’t benefit the nudged less ethically legitimate?

When nudgers wish to act with the interests of the nudged in mind (most probably do), an important [problem arises](https://doi.org/10.1111/phc3.12658): How can they know what is in the nudged’s best interest? This question is at the very heart of ethical critiques of nudging, especially with respect to paternalistic nudges. Nudgers may project or impose their own values and goals [onto others](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=1119325). At best, they assume that the targets of a nudge all have the same or context-independent preferences. Consider interventions that nudge people to take the stairs instead of the elevator. Some people may prefer to get their exercise elsewhere, are physically handicapped, or simply care more about convenience than health.

#### Addressing Concerns

In defense of nudging, it has been argued that having to [determine what’s right](https://doi.org/10.1177%2F1356389015590218) for targets of a policy isn’t exclusive to nudging and that individuals most susceptible to nudges tend to be those with [high uncertainty](https://doi.org/10.1177%2F0032321716629487) in the first place. Many practitioners have also adopted an [utilitarian view](https://texaslawreview.org/how-i-learned-to-stop-worrying-and-love-nudges/) of costs and benefits that focuses on bringing about the greatest good for the greatest number. (Covid-19 behaviors are a dramatic example.)

Richard Thaler, the father of nudge, maintains that practitioners should [“nudge for good”](https://www.nytimes.com/2015/11/01/upshot/the-power-of-nudges-for-good-and-bad.html). There should [also be](https://www.nytimes.com/2015/11/01/upshot/the-power-of-nudges-for-good-and-bad.html) “good reason to believe that the behavior being encouraged will improve the welfare of those being nudged.” It’s good practice to take a deep dive into data you may already have about your nudge targets or conduct new research to better understand their preferences. This is [not always an easy task](https://doi.org/10.1017/bpp.2019.53), though, since preferences can be uncertain, context-dependent, and variable over time.

When preferences are diverse, choice architects (especially those in the digital and/or the commercial world) can attempt to target different consumers better with [programs and interventions](https://www.behavioraleconomics.com/a-nudge-for-coverage-last-mile-problems-for-health-insurance/) that fit their preferences. To better target individuals, a bit of [data science](https://www.behavioraleconomics.com/behavioural-data-science-ushering-in-a-new-age/) can help. If you are unable to work with fancy algorithms (like Netflix or Amazon’s recommendation engines), traditional segmentation research should do the trick. Nudges to [increase savings rates](http://dx.doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.2735625), for example, may not fit the preferences of some low-income segments with liquidity constraints, who may be better served with other programs, such as helping them avoid high-interest loans.

| **Concern: Nudgers’ GOALS Don’t Always Match the Nudged’s Preferences** |
| --- |
| * Are we acting with the nudged’s best interests in mind? * How much do we know about their preferences? * Can we target different individuals or segments with programs and interventions that fit their preferences? |

### Autonomy (of the Nudged)

Ethical discussions often point out that nudges may exploit people’s [irrationality](http://eprints.lse.ac.uk/37055/), are [excessively convenient](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=2672970), or even manipulate folks on an unconscious level.

In order to differentiate degrees of conscious thought (see examples in table below), nudges have been [classified](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=2555337) as either Type 2 (affecting [reflective](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/320969370_Nudge_Concept_Effectiveness_and_Ethics) choice or triggering an active response) or Type 1 (the targeted outcome is more automatic or the nudged’s reaction more passive). The descriptive norm in the message “80% of our guests reuse their towels” is a Type 2 nudge, whereas room service not changing the towels unless requested by the guest is a Type 1 nudge.

It has also been argued that nudges sometimes lack [transparency](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/320969370_Nudge_Concept_Effectiveness_and_Ethics). In a transparent nudge, the intention behind it and the way it operates is more apparent to the person being nudged. While we can expect most people to hear and notice ambient music, its influence is more transparent if it’s played at an airport to lighten travelers’ moods than at a [wine store](https://www.acrwebsite.org/volumes/7467/volumes/v20/NA-20) to make shoppers not just feel good, but buy more premium wines.

|  | **Transparent Example** | **Non-Transparent Example** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Type 2** | Helping healthy food choices by color-coding food calorie labels | Selling more $40 meals by adding a $50 meal to the menu |
| **Type 1** | Discouraging speeding motorists with trompe-l’œil paintings of children playing on the street | Increasing organ donation rates by changing organ donations from opt-in to opt-out |

#### Addressing Concerns

[Thaler and Sunstein](https://yalebooks.yale.edu/book/9780300262285/nudge) have acknowledged that some nudges may operate outside of individuals’ awareness and suggested that it should be easy to opt out of a nudge. Preserving this freedom is a central part of ethical nudge practice. For example, if you offer a membership that renews automatically, you need to make sure customers can switch off renewal with the click of a mouse button rather than having to find it buried deep in the structure of your website.

It’s important to bear in mind that nudges are just one of many tools in the [toolbox](https://www.behavioraleconomics.com/supporting-decision-making-under-uncertainty-nudging-boosting-or-both/) of those seeking to achieve corporate or policy goals. In order to address autonomy concerns, it’s important for practitioners to consider the full range of interventions (traditional vs non-traditional, Type 1 vs 2, etc.) at their disposal. If you’d like to decrease food waste at your buffet, for example, make sure you also test pricing options (e.g. prices for different types of eaters) and rational persuasion (e.g. “Help us reduce food waste…”) alongside your relatively non-transparent nudge (e.g. introducing smaller plates). This will not only increase your chances of finding the most effective approach, but also give different approaches a fair shake.

Should you go as far as disclosing your nudge to increase transparency? Some argue that disclosing nudges is impractical and [transparency ‘in principle’](http://eprints.lse.ac.uk/37055/) might suffice. While it’s commonly assumed that nudges work best [“in the dark”](http://eprints.lse.ac.uk/37055/), some studies on defaults have found that disclosing the default (e.g. “Please consider that the preselected default value might have an influence on your decision”) does not significantly reduce the [effectiveness of the nudge](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.joep.2018.02.002).

Research on the effect of disclosures across different nudges is still limited and you may want to conduct your own tests. Disclosure would not only have beneficial effects on ethics, it may also elicit positive responses from the nudged.

| **Concern: Nudges Threaten Individuals’ AUTONOMY** |
| --- |
| * Is it easy for decision makers to opt out of the nudge (to preserve freedom of choice)? * Have we tested alternatives to nudges (traditional interventions) and different types of nudges (e.g. Type 1 vs 2, transparent vs non-transparent)? * Should we consider disclosing our nudge? |

### Effects (of the Nudges)

There is a growing number of case studies and data about the effectiveness of nudge interventions (see this 2019 [meta-analysis,](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.socec.2019.03.005) this [2020 research](https://www.nber.org/papers/w27594) and this [study from 2022](https://www.pnas.org/doi/full/10.1073/pnas.2107346118), for example). Critics point to a lack of evidence to-date showing a link between nudges and genuine behavior change with [long-term effects](http://eprints.lse.ac.uk/37055/). If a nudge succeeds at making me floss my teeth for a week, has it also successfully changed my preferences with regards to oral health?

There are various reasons why nudges may lead to unanticipated consequences as well, such as reactance ([responding negatively](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/314697052_Default-Switching_The_Hidden_Cost_of_Defaults) to influence attempts) and other backfire or boomerang effects. For example, research on the effect of [descriptive norms on energy use](https://doi.org/10.1177%2F1745691617693325) found increased consumption for those households whose past energy consumption was below the norm. Side effects may also occur. A nudge may reduce [water consumption](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.enpol.2013.01.021), for example, but inadvertently also increase electricity consumption.

#### Addressing Concerns

While backfire effects on the nudged population overall may become apparent with testing, differences across subgroups and side effects on non-targeted behaviors are less easy to spot. It’s good practice for nudgers to anticipate potential issues early by gaining an understanding of nudge targets’ backgrounds (e.g. in terms of their demographics, such as income, race, or gender), unique circumstances (e.g. life stage or employment status), and associated needs. Low-income individuals, mentioned previously, are a one of many examples of a group that may respond differently to nudges.

You can make an ethical assessment of your program by carefully weighing the benefits and costs of behavioral interventions, as the ones illustrated by the following cases:

| **Goal** | **Benefits** | **Costs** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Reducing energy consumption](https://doi.org/10.1177%2F1745691617693325) | Consumption decrease among above-average households | Consumption increase among below-average households |
| [Reducing water consumption](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.enpol.2013.01.021) | Water consumption decrease | Electricity consumption increase |
| [Increasing donations](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpubeco.2017.11.005) | Social well-being | Annoyance; unsubscribes from mailing list |
| [Reducing calorie consumption](http://journal.sjdm.org/18/18829/jdm18829.html) | Lower calorie choices for the average person | Feelings of guilt among low self-control persons |
| [Promoting healthy eating](https://doi.org/10.1002/ajae.12036) | Increase in healthy food purchases | Increase in food waste (perishables) |

In order to weigh costs against benefits, they need to be quantified. This is relatively easy for behaviors like energy use. Intangible costs, however, such as bad feelings about a diet nudge, are more difficult to assess. In addition, effects may carry costs for different stakeholders. [Reminder emails](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpubeco.2017.11.005), for example, can have both costs associated with the loss of subscribers to the organization and ‘annoyance costs’ for targeted individuals.

If you are unfamiliar with formal economic approaches, you may want to measure costs and benefits with some help from an economist, which would also allow you to account for so-called “welfare effects”. Economists typically measure these effects of nudges using [willingness-to-pay](https://online.hbs.edu/blog/post/willingness-to-pay). Analyses may identify costs that are easily overlooked, such as having to give up a pleasant activity. Direct effects of the nudge may also lead to costs, such as the “emotional tax” some experience as a result of [calorie-labeling](http://journal.sjdm.org/18/18829/jdm18829.html). If the average individual values a nudge’s benefits at $10 and its drawbacks at $11, you better rethink your approach.

Once an intervention is live, practitioners should continuously monitor processes and outcomes, which in turn allows them to spot unwanted effects. If your nudge is designed to increase sales of a product, for example, make sure you also monitor sales of other products, as well as customer feedback received through regular CRM channels.

| **Concern: Nudges May Produce Unintended (Negative) EFFECTS** |
| --- |
| * What do we know about the backgrounds, circumstances, and needs of our nudge targets that may lead to unexpected outcomes? * Have we weighed up the costs and benefits of our intervention? * Can we continuously monitor the processes and outcomes associated with our nudge program? |

### Conclusion

The application of nudges has become a popular practice to solve problems in both the public and the private sector. Many professionals and organizations are looking to take their work to the next level by building an ethically-literate and future-oriented nudge practice.

I recommend for practitioners to start any project that involves nudges by first defining ethical guidelines. The 3×3 questions provided in this article can serve as a nudge ethics audit or checklist. In the beginning of the project, have each team member reflect on the questions, which can then be discussed as a team. The three problem areas are also relevant at different [stages of a typical nudge project](https://www.oecd.org/gov/regulatory-policy/tools-and-ethics-for-applied-behavioural-insights-the-basic-toolkit-9ea76a8f-en.htm):

Each group of questions should be revisited as the project progresses through corresponding stages and new knowledge emerges. Once the project has been wrapped up, you can use the recorded responses to questions as a guide for future projects.

Just as nudges may not provide one-size-fits-all solutions, their ethical use may need to be refined across projects and over time. In this article, I hope to have provided a starting point for practitioners to think about and implement ethical nudges.

Want to learn more about ethics in applied behavioral science? Check out our course below!

![](https://www.behavioraleconomics.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/09/CABSE-Ad-6.png)

SUMMARY:
Ethical nudging tactics are gentle guides that shape choices towards positive outcomes, particularly in sustainability and healthy living, without forceful mandates . This approach respects individual autonomy while gently pushing towards choices that benefit both the person and the planet.

# Ethical Nudging Tactics

![The close up macro photograph presents a myriad of tiny translucent spheres, evocative of water droplets or bubbles, densely clustered over a soft, light beige surface. The background is a blurred continuation of this neutral hue, creating a harmonious and minimalist aesthetic](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/macro-photograph-showcases-gentle-water-droplets-illustrating-pure-sustainable.jpg)
![A single pillar candle dominates the frame, showcasing its warm gentle flame against a muted grey backdrop suggestive of a concrete or stone surface. The candle itself, rendered in soft focus, appears robust and cylindrical with a textured, natural wax finish that hints at sustainable materials and ethical production](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-natural-wax-candlelight-offering-eco-conscious-gentle-illumination.jpg)

## Fundamentals

Let’s start with what [Ethical Nudging Meaning → Guiding choices ethically for better living & a sustainable world.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/term/ethical-nudging/)  Tactics truly represents. At its most basic, consider it as a gentle guide, a friendly suggestion whispered in the ear of decision-making. It’s about shaping choices without forceful commands, akin to arranging the furniture in a room to naturally encourage movement in a certain direction. Think of it as the art of subtle steering, not shoving, particularly when we discuss building a more sustainable world and fostering healthier lifestyles.

This approach respects [individual autonomy Meaning → Individual autonomy is your power to make self-directed choices, vital for personal and planetary sustainability.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/term/individual-autonomy/)  while gently pushing towards choices that benefit both the person and the planet. It acknowledges that sometimes, our best intentions get lost in the chaos of daily life. We might genuinely want to recycle, eat healthier, or conserve energy, yet the immediate ease of less sustainable options can often prevail. Ethical nudging steps in to bridge this gap, making the virtuous path a little bit smoother, a little bit more appealing, and a little bit more… well, human.

![This close up sepia toned image showcases a collection of frothy soap bubbles tightly grouped together creating a dense texture. The lighting highlights the rounded shapes and varying sizes of the bubbles, emphasizing their delicate and transient nature](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/gentle-eco-friendly-foam-for-sustainable-cleansing-a-close-up-view-of-natural.jpg)

### The Human Touch in Sustainable Choices

Why “ethical” nudging? The ethical dimension is **paramount**. It’s the compass that directs these subtle tactics away from manipulation and towards genuine support. [Ethical nudges Meaning → Gentle guidance towards better choices without limiting freedom.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/term/ethical-nudges/)  operate in the light, with transparency and respect.

They aim to empower individuals to make better choices for themselves and the collective good, not to trick them into decisions they might later regret. It’s about alignment with core values of fairness, honesty, and respect for personal agency.

Imagine a cafeteria setting.

These are everyday illustrations of ethical nudging in action. They work because they align with our inherent desire for ease and convenience, while gently guiding us toward more beneficial paths. They understand human psychology – that we are often influenced by our immediate surroundings and the way options are presented to us.

![A picturesque view unfolds showcasing a field abundant with delicate pink blossom trees, epitomizing natural beauty and promoting an eco-conscious lifestyle. The image is centered on a serene landscape where soft pink blossoms create a visually calming effect](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/gentle-pink-blossom-trees-in-natural-field-showcasing-beauty-of-sustainable-and.jpg)

### Nudging towards a Sustainable Lifestyle

Sustainability, in its truest form, isn’t about deprivation or sacrifice. It’s about intelligent living, about designing our lives in ways that support both our well-being and the health of our planet. Ethical nudging provides a powerful toolkit for realizing this vision. It recognizes that large-scale shifts in behavior often begin with small, incremental changes in individual habits.

Consider these scenarios:

These examples showcase how ethical nudging operates at a systems level, shaping environments to make sustainable choices the default, the easier, and often, the more desirable options. It’s about creating a world where living sustainably is not a chore, but a natural, almost unconscious, aspect of daily life.

![A woman with fair skin and prominent freckles on her face, neck, and shoulders is depicted with her eyes gently closed. She has subtle black winged eyeliner](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/freckled-womans-minimalist-portrait-with-closed-eyes-against-soft-pink-hues.jpg)
![A tranquil minimalist landscape unfolds featuring rolling hills cloaked in natural dry grasses, softly illuminated by a pale sunrise. The sky exhibits a gradient from pale pink to off white, showcasing the sun as a gentle, luminous disc on the horizon line](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/gentle-pathways-across-rolling-hills-under-soft-sunrise-evoking-sustainable.jpg)

## Intermediate

Moving beyond the foundational understanding, let’s examine the more complex dimensions of Ethical Nudging Tactics. We now understand the gentle steering, but what fuels this approach? It’s a deep understanding of **behavioral science**, the intricate workings of the human mind, and the subtle cues that influence our decisions, often without our conscious awareness. This is not about simple manipulation; instead, it is about architecting choice environments that resonate with our inherent human tendencies towards ease, social connection, and aspiration.

![A light orange tabby cat with striking turquoise eyes rests calmly on a rough-textured concrete ground outdoors. Soft, diffused natural light illuminates the scene, creating gentle shadows that enhance the feline’s form](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/gentle-orange-tabby-cat-relaxing-in-natural-light-a-moment-of-simple-joy-and.jpg)

### The Psychology of the Nudge → Understanding Human Behavior

At the heart of ethical nudging lies the recognition that humans are not perfectly rational actors. Traditional economic models often assume we make decisions based purely on logic and self-interest. However, [behavioral science Meaning → Behavioral science, in the context of sustainability, refers to the systematic analysis of human actions and decision-making processes that impact environmental and social systems.](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/area/behavioral-science/)  reveals a richer, more human picture.

We are influenced by cognitive biases, emotional responses, and social contexts. Nudges work by leveraging these very human traits, not by attempting to override them.

Key psychological principles at play include:

| Principle | Description | Application in Ethical Nudging |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Defaults** | People tend to stick with pre-set options. | Making sustainable choices the default option, such as automatic enrollment in green energy programs. |
| **Framing** | The way information is presented significantly impacts decisions. | Highlighting the gains of sustainable behavior rather than the losses of unsustainable actions. |
| **Social Norms** | People are influenced by what they perceive as normal behavior in their social group. | Communicating the prevalence of sustainable practices to encourage wider adoption. |
| **Loss Aversion** | People feel the pain of a loss more strongly than the pleasure of an equivalent gain. | Framing inaction on sustainability as a potential loss (e.g., loss of natural resources for future generations). |
| **Present Bias** | People tend to prioritize immediate rewards over future consequences. | Emphasizing the immediate benefits of sustainable actions, such as cost savings or improved local environment. |

Ethical nudging harnesses these principles responsibly. It doesn’t exploit vulnerabilities, but rather, it works with the grain of human nature to guide us towards better outcomes. The ethics lies precisely in this intention → to facilitate informed choices that are genuinely in the individual’s and society’s best interest.

![Transparent bubbles are densely clustered on a textured surface that is distinctly divided by color. To the left, a rough gray brown surface contrasts sharply with the right side displaying a lighter pink and white texture, possibly a natural material](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/gentle-bubbles-embrace-natural-textures-for-a-pure-eco-conscious-lifestyle-and.jpg)

### Ethical Boundaries → Transparency, Choice Architecture, and Autonomy

The term “ethical” is not merely an adjective here; it is the very foundation of this approach. Several crucial ethical considerations guide the application of nudging tactics:

When these ethical boundaries are respected, nudging becomes a powerful instrument for positive change. It becomes a way to support individuals in aligning their actions with their aspirations, particularly in the complex domain of sustainable living.

Consider the application of nudging in promoting sustainable consumption.

These tactics, when implemented ethically, contribute to a more sustainable society by empowering individuals to make conscious, values-driven choices in their daily lives.

![The image presents a pale delicate snake its scales intricately textured resting peacefully on a rough untreated wooden branch. The almost monochrome palette with subtle variations of beige and cream enhances the sense of serenity and natural harmony](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/a-pale-snake-embodiment-of-serene-natural-lifestyle-a-gentle-touch-of.jpg)
![A macro close-up reveals a textured, beige surface characterized by small, rounded protuberances and delicate water droplets. The material appears porous and soft, suggesting an organic and natural composition aligned with sustainable living principles](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/gentle-sustainable-cleansing-texture-natural-eco-friendly-material-with-water.jpg)

## Academic

At the expert level, Ethical Nudging Tactics invite rigorous scrutiny, moving beyond practical application into the realm of philosophical debate, societal impact analysis, and cross-cultural considerations. From an academic perspective, ethical nudging is not a monolithic concept, but a subject of ongoing discourse, defined by its inherent tensions → the tension between **agency and influence**, between individual liberty and collective well-being, and between behavioral science insights and diverse ethical frameworks.

![The soft focused image features slender, dried flower stems with delicate seed heads emerging from a misty, gentle haze, embodying an eco-friendly and serene aesthetic. The muted earthy tones and minimalist composition evoke a sense of organic simplicity, highlighting the natural beauty inherent in sustainable living](https://lifestyle.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/delicate-beauty-of-dried-flowers-embracing-sustainable-and-gentle-harmony-with.jpg)

### Redefining Ethical Nudging → A Multi-Dimensional Perspective

A rigorous academic definition of Ethical Nudging Tactics moves beyond simple behavioral interventions. Introduction We are not as rational as we think. While we like to think that our decisions are the result of rational reflection, the truth is that things we are unaware of actually influence our decisions all the time. Advances in cognitive and behavioral science reveal that the way that options are presented, what is referred to as the ‘choice architecture’, strongly influences our decisions. We tend to react to a particular option differently depending on how it is presented to us. Based on this data, Richard H. Thaler and Cass R. Sunstein came up with nudging, the idea that people’s decisions and behaviors should be influenced in predictable, non-coercive ways by making small changes to the choice architecture. Central to the debate about nudging is the question of whether it is morally permissible to intentionally nudge other people. Libertarian paternalists maintain that this can be the case and offer a criteria for the moral permissibility of intentional interventions. I start this project by presenting clear and coherent accounts of Nudging and Libertarian Paternalism that are faithful to how these concepts were originally introduced — PANDION: The Osprey Journal of Research and Ideas, Vol. 1 No. 2, 2020 — Valerie Joly Chock — 2 — by Thaler and Sunstein. My purpose in doing this is to dispel two misconceptions often found in the literature: (i) that nudges are intentional and avoidable, and (ii) that all instances of nudging are interventions grounded by Libertarian Paternalism. By clearing up the confusion surrounding these misconceptions, my project is able to move away from the question of whether it can be morally permissible to nudge, to more productive discussion about how it is permissible to do so. To answer this question, I present the Libertarian Paternalistic Criteria (LPC) for the moral permissibility of intentional nudges and defend it against two objections: Biased Choice Architects and The Manipulation Objection. The former objects to the moral permissibility of intentional nudging in general, whereas the latter objects only to a particular subset of nudges. While I show that both objections fail, I argue that the evaluation of The Manipulation Objection raises a different challenge for LPC. The Libertarian Paternalistic Criteria for the moral permissibility of intentional nudges fails because it ignores the moral distinction that exists between different kinds of nudges. Such distinction is not the one proposed by the objection, but one between what I call ‘counteractive’ and ‘non-counteractive’ nudges. I end by suggesting a possible revision to the criteria that avoids the problem, considering its limitations, and providing examples of its applications. 1. NUDGING In their book Nudge: Improving Decisions About Health, Wealth, and Happiness, Richard Thaler and Cass Sunstein define a ‘nudge’ as “any aspect of the choice architecture that alters people’s behavior in a predictable way without forbidding any options or significantly changing their economic incentives.”1 In other words, a nudge is a change in the way options are presented that steers people in a certain direction without forbidding any options, all while maintaining people’s freedom of choice. Suggestions, warnings, defaults, and recommendations are all examples of common nudges. On the other hand, fines, mandates, threats, bans, and direct instruction are not, since they either forbid certain options or significantly reduce one’s freedom of choice. LIBERTARIAN PATERNALISM Libertarian Paternalism concerns how options should be presented. It is motivated, in part, by the unavoidability of choice architecture. When options are presented, they simply must be presented in some way or other. Further, any way of presenting the options inevitably influences what people choose. According to libertarian paternalists, this, coupled with the fact that people often make irrational decisions, can make intentional nudges morally permissible. Since nudging will inevitably occur, choice architects should nudge well. However, an intentional intervention is not morally permissible merely in virtue of being a nudge. Libertarian paternalists propose a criteria for the moral permissibility of intentional interventions (hereafter ‘LPC’), which gives a set of sufficient conditions for the moral permissibility of an intentional intervention. According to LPC, any intervention that is (i) a nudge, (ii) transparent, and (iii) aimed towards the welfare of those being nudged is morally permissible.32 Here, (i) requires that the intervention must preserve freedom of choice—it cannot forbid or attach incentives or consequences to options33; (ii) requires that the influence must be easy to resist—people must be able to choose a different available option34; and (iii) requires that the intervention must be justifiably intended to make people better off—it must have people’s best interest in mind. 35  Thaler and Sunstein recognize that “Libertarian Paternalism” seems to be a contradiction in terms. However, they “unsettle the conventional wisdom” by arguing that this term is a conceptual possibility. See Thaler and Sunstein (2003).
The Ethics and Applications of Nudges — 11 — To see LPC applied to a case, consider the following example: Cafeteria Jess is a cafeteria manager who decides the way in which food is displayed. After reading some psychology studies, she learns that the food order at a cafeteria line substantially determines what ends on diners’ plates—the majority of people tend to select the food that is placed first in line.36 Aware of this, Jess now knows that whatever she decides regarding food placement will influence her diners’ choices. So, she asks herself: “How should I arrange the food?”37 Jess could arrange the food: (a) So that diners are better off;38 (b) at random; (c) to maximize her profits; (d) by banning all unhealthy foods. No matter which option she chooses, her decision will inevitably influence the diners’ decisions. The fact that food must be arranged in some way is unescapable. Option (d) limits the available options, so it fails to meet (i) of LPC. Thus, in this case, option (d) is not a permissible intervention under LPC.39 The remaining options (a–c), all meet condition (ii) of LPC because they do not make it significantly more difficult for diners to choose any of the other available food. However, not all (a–c) satisfy (iii). Option (b) fails to satisfy (iii) because to arrange the food at random is to consciously ignore the diners’ best interests. Option (c) fails to satisfy (iii) because it either reflects the architect’s selfish purposes or fails to even consider the diners’ welfare. Therefore, from Jess’ set of options, (a) is the only option justified by LPC—and, thus, (a) is morally permissible. Cafeteria also allows us to see that there is a distinction between instances of nudging and interventions grounded by LPC. The former is a change in the choice

15 Examples of workplace nudging
================================

[Trends in HR](https://www.hrtrendinstitute.com/blog/category/Trends+in+HR)

9 Nov

Written By [Tom Haak](https://www.hrtrendinstitute.com/blog?author=64d7600788b5e3679c2e11b8)

What is nudging?
----------------

Wikipedia defines nudging as follows:

> “Nudge is a concept in behavioural science,political theory and economics which proposes positive reinforcement and indirect suggestions as ways to influence the behaviour and decision making of groups or individuals. Nudging contrasts with other ways to achieve compliance, such as education, legislation or enforcement”

Thaler & Sunstein, in their book [Nudge](https://www.amazon.com/Nudge-Improving-Decisions-Health-Happiness/dp/014311526X), give the following definition:

> "Any aspect of the choice architecture that alters people's behavior in a predictable way without forbidding any options or significantly changing their economic incentives... To count as a mere nudge, the intervention must be easy and cheap to avoid. Nudges are not mandates. Putting the fruit at eye level counts as a nudge. Banning junk food does not."

According to [Thaler](https://www.nytimes.com/2015/11/01/upshot/the-power-of-nudges-for-good-and-bad.html), three principles should guide the use of nudges:

*   All nudges should be transparent and never misleading

*   It should be as easy as possible to opt out of the nudge

*   There should be good reason to believe that the behaviour being encouraged will improve the welfare of those being nudged.

The [Behavioral Insights Team](https://www.behaviouralinsights.co.uk/wp-content/uploads/2015/07/BIT-Publication-EAST_FA_WEB.pdf) use the EAST acronym when it comes to effectively stimulating behavioural change:

*   **E**asy

*   **A**ttractive

*   **S**ocial

*   **T**imely

Examples of workplace nudging
-----------------------------

### 1. 🍽 Smaller plates

Desired behaviour: Eat less.

Nudge: Give people smaller plates, and they will eat less. Easy to apply at the company restaurant, less applicable in remote working situations, although you could send your employees a small "Thank You" plate and hope they use it for eating.

![Image 3](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/e48ea0ce-997d-40b3-8fa4-6d041c47720d/miti-6ZidnWu4h3w-unsplash-300x208.jpg)

![Image 4](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/56abb729-91e4-4819-b7ef-74dd6b5c9025/skivis_32_thank_you_01-800x600.jpg)

### 2. 🏝 Office design: create visual islands

Desired behaviour: keep distance.

Nudge: Put workstations on dark carpet. People will have the tendency not to step on the dark carpet, and so naturally keep the desired distance (example from [Cushman & Wakefield](https://www.cushmanwakefield.com/en/netherlands/six-feet-office)).

![Image 5](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/24499b12-44c7-4910-a78a-effe6466a861/office-nudging.png)

### 3. 🗑 Remove all but one waste baskets

![Image 6](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/c8621ad6-2c9c-493e-a1ff-392d482150bd/steve-johnson-Kr8Tc8Rugdk-unsplash-360x240.jpg)

Desired behaviour: people move regularly.

Nudge: Remove all wastebaskets but one from the floor. Put one waste basket in the far-away corner so that everybody has to walk when they want to throw something away. Lazy people will produce less waste, and that’s also ok, of course.

### 4. 🪜 Take the stairs please

Desired behaviour: More walking

Nudge: (Re)design your office so that the stairs are dominant, and the elevators are hardly visible and difficult to find. People tend to choose the default option, so make sure stairs are default.

![Image 7](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/fff537b1-dbb3-497d-961c-043b6d0d2505/9F3E8B85-ED05-4476-BC1C-08DE792E5DF0-300x225.jpeg)

### 5. 🪰 Fly (or goal) in urinal

Desired behaviour: Pee inside the urinal.

Nudge: Put something in the urinal men like to aim at. A fly or a goal work, but with some imagination, you can think of many other solutions.

Read: Blake Evans-Pritchard: [Aiming to reduce costs](https://worksthatwork.com/1/urinal-fly)

![Image 8](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/993bc91f-de95-41fb-84e2-7133517a77cf/N80-0456.jpg)

![Image 9](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/9ef75fc2-be38-4cc6-b941-06286771cc09/ibifWpw.jpeg)

### 6. 💥 Casual collisions (or serendipitous encounters)

![Image 10](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/8ffd39c5-4942-4ade-abef-f454e939e847/Zappos-3-023A.jpg)

Desired behaviour: Meet some new people, outside your "bubble".

Nudge: Choose a lively location for your HQ. Zappos one moved their HQ to downtown Las Vegas. Because of the location the changes that your employees meet new people are increased. This is not a cheap nudge. You might start, if you still use offices, by mixing departments.

Read:

*   [The new Zappos downtown Las Vegas headquarters](https://officesnapshots.com/2013/12/16/new-zappos-downtown-las-vegas-headquarters/)

*   Waber, Magnolfi & Lindsay: [Workplaces that move people](https://hbr.org/2014/10/workspaces-that-move-people)

### 7. 😷 Display portraits of ethical leaders

Desired behaviour: Ethical behaviour.

Nudge: Put pictures of people who are perceived as ethical leaders on the walls or on the desktop of the computers. Research has shown that this will stimulate people who engage in less unethical behaviour. I won't mention examples; please make your own list.

Source: Re:Work editors: [Nudge people to encourage ethical behavior](https://rework.withgoogle.com/blog/nudge-people-to-encourage-ethical-behavior/)

![Image 11](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/387b5497-6ee6-44e5-93b4-e97aa24d410f/claude-piche-3tCaM1j9Z54-unsplash-200x300.jpg)

### 8. 🖨 Make printing difficult

Desired behaviour: Printing less, walking more.

Nudge: Remove all the printers but one from the floor or office. Everybody can use this printer.

Read: [9 sneaky ways to get employees to print less](https://www.laserfiche.com/ecmblog/9-sneaky-ways-to-get-employees-to-print-less/)

![Image 12](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/1691947986057-O1T2XQLOQAORRS782BLN/nudging-printer.jpg)

### 9. 🍎 Fruit on eye level

![Image 13](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/1691947985500-BOMAK29G5ZJ81QSL24UI/nudging-apple.jpg)

Desired behaviour: Eating healthy snacks (like fruit).

Nudge: Make fruit and other snacks easily accessible and put them where they are clearly visible. Put the unhealthy snacks high or low in the cupboard. People still have a choice, but there is a friendly nudge in the desired direction.

### 10. 🏡 Provide flexible desks at home

![Image 14](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/1691947985961-4UZI8VIY76ZJY19FZAED/nudging-desk.jpg)

Desired behaviour: Less sitting, more standing.

Nudge: Provide people with a flexible desk, at home or in the office. At this desk, you can alternate sitting and standing. Is this a nudge? Maybe the desk should ask now and then: "Do you want me to move to the standing position?".

### 11. 🥎 Dress in tracksuit

![Image 15](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/1691947986671-5CLB3X3VODWSOHKZARYA/nudging-training.jpg)

Desired behaviour: More exercise.

Nudge: Ask some of your employees to wear their sportswear in the office or during video calls. Other people might think: this is a good idea, especially if opinion leaders give a good example.

### 12. 👐 Washing hands

Desired behaviour: Regularly washing hands.

Nudge: Include a picture of hands that are washed at the beginning of your presentations, or make it your Zoom/Teams background.

![Image 16](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/1691947987371-18Z6DJZC30ZYD1Z60V6Y/nudging-waskhing-hands.jpg)

### 13. 🐾 Show the way with footsteps

![Image 17](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/1691947985370-JZTXXSXN8E7LD7EHN4JJ/nudging-6.jpg)

Desired behaviour: Follow a certain route in the office.

Nudge: Paint footsteps on the floor, and people will have the tendency to follow those steps.

### 14. 🚶🚶🏽‍♀️Walking meeting?

![Image 18](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/2ab794c1-6327-434a-8af3-d9c50e5d785b/sitting-meeting.jpg)

![Image 19](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/73fc7620-6789-469b-adba-506f6acb13fa/standing-meeting.jpg)

![Image 20](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/8c23d3a8-0ea4-4211-b249-81963891b0e1/remote-meeting.jpg)

![Image 21](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/88a5351c-df2b-479d-a828-2b414defe049/walking-meeting.jpg)

Desired behaviour: Walk more

Nudge: When people want to schedule a meeting, ask them what kind of meeting they want (Traditional, Standing, Video or Walking). Add "Most Popular" or "Chose by most people" to the Walking Meeting option.

### 15. 🐶 🐶🐶🐶🐶 Add a puppy

![Image 22](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/1691947984764-2ABQ835BSI7JGU4NL1X9/nudging-10.jpg)

Desired behaviour: People feel good (not really a behaviour, but it might lead to some nice behaviours).

Nudge: Show a puppy. Real puppies are great, but pictures of puppies can also work. The puppies can be used everywhere (postcards, screensavers, posters).

![Image 23](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/1691947973263-DO4JT2M3CK91B8JZH234/IMG_9007.jpg)

### 16. 😊 Smiley on the mirror

![Image 24](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/1691947972452-65JPF3LWRXZI13NQPTU2/IMG_8993.jpeg)

Desired behaviour: More smiles.

Nudge: Stick a smiley on the mirror. Can be done at home and work. Send a smiley to all your staff. Put a smiley on your Zoom/Teams background.(😁 [Erik Maas](https://www.linkedin.com/in/erik-maas/))

### 💡17. Blue Light

![Image 25](https://images.squarespace-cdn.com/content/v1/64d75f1d88b5e3679c2dfa3e/af84ecc3-3230-4f71-a413-3c553d75c73a/daniele-levis-pelusi-ktSzoJaNDMM-unsplash.jpg)

Desired behaviour: More productive behaviour

Nudge: Provide cool, blue light. I am not sure this qualifies as a nudge, but if it works, who cares. You could provide your employees with an LCD lamp for at home.

Read:

Mills, Tomkins & Schlangen: [The effect of high correlated colour temperature office lighting on employee wellbeing and work performance](https://www.jcircadianrhythms.com/articles/10.1186/1740-3391-5-2/)

(😁 [Marieke van der Giessen](https://www.linkedin.com/in/mariekevandergiessen/))

enriched_points.expanded_content: Ethical nudging involves guiding choices without manipulation, preserving autonomy, and ensuring that interventions genuinely benefit individuals, aligning with their best interests and societal values.

Transparency in nudging requires clear communication of intentions, easy opt-out options, and making the mechanics of nudges apparent to those being influenced, fostering trust and informed decision-making.

enriched_points.point_title: How to involve stakeholders in decision-making?
enriched_points.expanded_content: Engaging stakeholders in the nudging process promotes diverse perspectives, enhances acceptance, and ensures that the interventions address real needs, aligning with ethical standards and community values.
